selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
No empty fields nor ... are allowed. Do not skip any field.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":73,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Milan","employment_status":"unemployed","education_level":"upper secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"PhD","parent_2":"upper secondary"},"current_marital_status":"single","children":2,"migration_status":"native‑born Italian","psychological_feature":"curious","religious​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​…} }{" :"}{" } ,"opinion":"I have spent many evenings in my small apartment in Milan scrolling through the endless stream of posts, and I have learned that dealing with fake content is not a matter of simply ignoring it or trusting my instincts alone. The first step for someone like me—an elderly man who spends several hours daily on social media—is to cultivate a habit of questioning every headline before reacting. When a post catches my eye, I pause and ask: Who posted this? What evidence does the author provide? Is there a link to an original source that can be verified? Even if the article looks convincing at first glance, it is worth checking whether reputable news outlets have reported on the same story. If no credible outlet has covered it, that raises a red flag. Secondly, I use tools that are designed for the average user: browser extensions such as “NewsGuard” or “Fake News Detector” give quick credibility ratings based on fact‑checking databases. These tools do not replace critical thinking but they provide an extra layer of safety. Thirdly, I rely on my curiosity—a trait that keeps me engaged with new technologies—to explore the background of a claim. If a post mentions a scientific study, I search for the paper in Google Scholar or PubMed; if it cites a government report, I go directly to the official website. This habit has helped me spot misinformation about health treatments and political events that were circulating on my feed. Fourthly, I practice digital hygiene by limiting the amount of time I spend on feeds that are known for sensational content. Instead of scrolling aimlessly, I set specific times—say 30 minutes in the morning and 30 minutes in the evening—to check news from trusted sources like Corriere della Sera or La Repubblica. This reduces exposure to clickbait and gives me a structured approach to staying informed. Fifthly, when I encounter fake content that has already spread widely, I take responsibility by sharing a brief correction. I do not feel pressured to become an expert; simply adding a short note such as “I found this claim is unverified according to the Italian National Institute of Health” can help my friends and family avoid falling for the same misinformation. Finally, I remind myself that emotional reactions—especially fear or anger—can cloud judgment. When a post triggers a strong feeling, I step back, breathe, and give myself time before deciding whether to engage. By combining skepticism, verification tools, curiosity-driven research, controlled exposure, corrective sharing, and emotional regulation, I have found a practical framework that works for me as an older adult who still wants to stay connected without becoming a victim of fake content.","reasoning_summary":"1. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: I have spent many evenings in my small apartment in Milan scrolling through the endless stream of posts, and I have learned that dealing with fake content is not a matter of simply ignoring it or trusting my instincts alone. The first step for someone like me—an elderly man who spends several hours daily on social media—is to cultivate a habit of questioning every headline before reacting. When a post catches my eye, I pause and ask: Who posted this? What evidence does the author provide? Is there a link to an original source that can be verified? Even if the article looks convincing at first glance, it is worth checking whether reputable news outlets have reported on the same story. If no credible outlet has covered it, that raises a red flag. Secondly, I use tools that are designed for the average user: browser extensions such as “NewsGuard” or “Fake News Detector” give quick credibility ratings based on fact‑checking databases. These tools do not replace critical thinking but they provide an extra layer of safety. Thirdly, I rely on my curiosity—a trait that keeps me engaged with new technologies—to explore the background of a claim. If a post mentions a scientific study, I search for the paper in Google Scholar or PubMed; if it cites a government report, I go directly to the official website. This habit has helped me spot misinformation about health treatments and political events that were circulating on my feed. Fourthly, I practice digital hygiene by limiting the amount of time I spend on feeds that are known for sensational content. Instead of scrolling aimlessly, I set specific times—say 30 minutes in the morning and 30 minutes in the evening—to check news from trusted sources like Corriere della Sera or La Repubblica. This reduces exposure to clickbait and gives me a structured approach to staying informed. Fifthly, when I encounter fake content that has already spread widely, I take responsibility by sharing a brief correction. I do not feel pressured to become an expert; simply adding a short note such as “I found this claim is unverified according to the Italian National Institute of Health” can help my friends and family avoid falling for the same misinformation. Finally, I remind myself that emotional reactions—especially fear or anger—can cloud judgment. When a post triggers a strong feeling, I step back, breathe, and give myself time before deciding whether to engage. By combining skepticism, verification tools, curiosity-driven research, controlled exposure, corrective sharing, and emotional regulation, I have found a practical framework that works for me as an older adult who still wants to stay connected without becoming a victim of fake content.
